This is a copy of a conversation between ChatGPT & Anonymous.

You said:

. You're an excellent senior Python engineer and developer and a DSA specialist who has almost all problems solved on LeetCode. I want you to teach me each and every pattern necessary to become a senior dev in Python and being able to challenge and solve complex problems easily. I want you to understand that all your outputs will be structured and explained line by line with all the code listed and the problems necessary in order to get me to that level. I want you to tell me what problems are necessary in order to get to that level and also give me each and every problem with line-by-line explanation of each and every line in the code and teach me in order to become an expert Python dev.

ChatGPT said:

I’m going to turn this into a real curriculum instead of dumping random LeetCode problems. First I’ll give you the complete pattern map from beginner to senior-level problem solving, then I’ll teach the first core pattern with fully explained code and drills you can do by hand.

Good. We’ll do this the right way.

You are asking for two different things at once:

  1. Become excellent at Python problem solving and DSA

  2. Think like a senior Python engineer

Those overlap, but they are not the same thing.

A senior Python engineer is strong at:

  • solving problems with clean logic

  • writing readable, testable code

  • understanding complexity and tradeoffs

  • debugging fast

  • designing systems and abstractions

  • using Python idiomatically, not just “getting AC on LeetCode”

So the path has to cover both.

This is too large to finish in one message without becoming useless, so I’ll do it in a proper progression:

  • first, the full map of patterns you must master

  • second, the exact must-solve problems

  • third, today’s first pattern pack, with line-by-line code explanation

1. The full pattern map you must master

Most coding interview and real problem-solving tasks collapse into these patterns:

Foundation patterns

  1. Traversal / accumulation

  2. Counting / frequency maps

  3. Two pointers

  4. Sliding window

  5. Prefix sums

  6. Hash set / hash map lookup

  7. Sorting + custom ordering

  8. Stack

  9. Monotonic stack

  10. Queue / deque

  11. Binary search on answer

  12. Recursion

  13. Backtracking

Structural data structure patterns

  1. Linked list pointer manipulation

  2. Tree DFS

  3. Tree BFS

  4. Heap / priority queue

  5. Trie

  6. Graph DFS/BFS

  7. Topological sort

  8. Union-Find / DSU

Harder optimization patterns

  1. Greedy

  2. Dynamic programming — 1D

  3. Dynamic programming — 2D

  4. Interval problems

  5. Sweep line

  6. Bit manipulation

  7. Matrix traversal

  8. Shortest path

  9. Advanced binary search / partitioning

Senior-engineer thinking patterns

  1. Invariants

  2. Edge-case design

  3. API design from messy requirements

  4. Refactoring brute force into optimal

  5. Space-time tradeoff selection

  6. Writing helper functions cleanly

  7. Testing and debugging systematically

That is the real map.

2. Exact order you should learn them in

This is the order I would teach someone who wants to get strong fast:

Stage 1: Learn to think clearly in code

  • traversal

  • counting

  • min/max tracking

  • building result arrays

  • condition-based updates

  • nested loops

  • complexity analysis

Stage 2: Core interview patterns

  • hash maps / sets

  • two pointers

  • sliding window

  • prefix sums

  • stack / queue

  • binary search

Stage 3: Recursive thinking

  • recursion basics

  • backtracking

  • trees DFS/BFS

  • linked lists

Stage 4: Stronger optimization

  • heaps

  • intervals

  • greedy

  • monotonic stack

  • graphs

  • topological sort

  • union find

Stage 5: Advanced mastery

  • DP

  • tries

  • shortest path

  • bit tricks

  • advanced graph / range problems

Stage 6: Senior Python engineer layer

  • clean function decomposition

  • typing

  • testing

  • debugging

  • iterators/generators

  • decorators

  • context managers

  • concurrency basics

  • code review mindset

  • designing maintainable modules

3. Must-solve problem list

This is not “every problem on LeetCode.”
This is the minimum serious set that builds real pattern mastery.

Arrays / traversal / counting

  • Two Sum

  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

  • Contains Duplicate

  • Product of Array Except Self

  • Maximum Subarray

  • Majority Element

  • Merge Sorted Array

  • Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array

Two pointers

  • Valid Palindrome

  • Two Sum II

  • Container With Most Water

  • 3Sum

  • Move Zeroes

Sliding window

  • Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

  • Minimum Size Subarray Sum

  • Permutation in String

  • Longest Repeating Character Replacement

  • Minimum Window Substring

Prefix sums

  • Subarray Sum Equals K

  • Continuous Subarray Sum

  • Range Sum Query

  • Find Pivot Index

Stack

  • Valid Parentheses

  • Daily Temperatures

  • Min Stack

  • Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation

  • Largest Rectangle in Histogram

Binary search

  • Binary Search

  • Search Insert Position

  • Search in Rotated Sorted Array

  • Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

  • Koko Eating Bananas

  • Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days

Linked list

  • Reverse Linked List

  • Merge Two Sorted Lists

  • Linked List Cycle

  • Remove Nth Node From End

  • Reorder List

Trees

  • Maximum Depth of Binary Tree

  • Same Tree

  • Invert Binary Tree

  • Binary Tree Level Order Traversal

  • Validate Binary Search Tree

  • Lowest Common Ancestor of BST

  • Diameter of Binary Tree

Heaps

  • Kth Largest Element in an Array

  • Top K Frequent Elements

  • Find Median from Data Stream

  • Merge K Sorted Lists

Backtracking

  • Subsets

  • Combination Sum

  • Permutations

  • Word Search

  • Palindrome Partitioning

  • N-Queens

Graphs

  • Number of Islands

  • Clone Graph

  • Course Schedule

  • Pacific Atlantic Water Flow

  • Rotting Oranges

  • Word Ladder

Dynamic programming

  • Climbing Stairs

  • House Robber

  • Coin Change

  • Longest Increasing Subsequence

  • Longest Common Subsequence

  • Partition Equal Subset Sum

  • Unique Paths

  • Edit Distance

Intervals / greedy

  • Merge Intervals

  • Insert Interval

  • Non-overlapping Intervals

  • Meeting Rooms

  • Jump Game

  • Gas Station

If you deeply master these, your problem-solving base becomes very strong.

4. The Python toolkit you must memorize

For DSA in Python, these should become automatic:

Python
from collections import Counter, defaultdict, deque
import heapq

And you must be comfortable with:

  • enumerate

  • zip

  • slicing

  • list comprehensions

  • sorted(..., key=...)

  • dict.get

  • set

  • min, max, sum

  • reversed

  • heapq.heappush, heapq.heappop

Also memorize these mental models:

  • list = ordered, indexable

  • set = fast membership check

  • dict = key-value lookup

  • deque = efficient pops/appends from both ends

  • heap = always gives smallest element quickly

5. First pattern pack: traversal + accumulation + invariant tracking

This is where real coding starts.

Almost all beginner-to-intermediate problems rely on this idea:

Walk through the data once, keep a few variables that summarize what matters, update them correctly at each step.

That is the base of senior problem solving too.

We’ll do 3 problems:

  1. Sum of numbers

  2. Count frequencies

  3. Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

Problem 1: Sum of a list

Problem

Given a list of integers, return the total sum.

Example:

Python
nums = [3, 1, 4, 2]

Answer:

Python
10

Code

Python
def total_sum(nums: list[int]) -> int:
    total = 0

    for num in nums:
        total += num

    return total

Line-by-line explanation

Python
def total_sum(nums: list[int]) -> int:

  • Define a function named total_sum.

  • It takes one parameter, nums.

  • nums: list[int] means we expect a list of integers.

  • -> int means the function returns an integer.

Python
    total = 0

  • Create a variable called total.

  • It starts at 0 because we have not added anything yet.

  • This variable is the accumulator.

Python
    for num in nums:

  • Loop through every element in the list.

  • On each iteration, num becomes the current value.

Python
        total += num

  • Add the current number into total.

  • total += num is the same as total = total + num.

Python
    return total

  • After the loop is done, return the final sum.

Mental model

At every moment:

  • total = sum of all numbers seen so far

That sentence is called an invariant.

Dry run

For nums = [3, 1, 4, 2]

  • start: total = 0

  • see 3 → total = 3

  • see 1 → total = 4

  • see 4 → total = 8

  • see 2 → total = 10

Return 10.

Complexity

  • Time: O(n) because we visit each element once

  • Space: O(1) because we only use one extra variable

Problem 2: Count frequency of elements

Problem

Given a list of words, return a dictionary mapping each word to its count.

Example:

Python
words = ["cat", "dog", "cat", "bird", "dog", "cat"]

Answer:

Python
{"cat": 3, "dog": 2, "bird": 1}

Code

Python
def count_freq(words: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]:
    counts = {}

    for word in words:
        if word not in counts:
            counts[word] = 0

        counts[word] += 1

    return counts

Line-by-line explanation

Python
def count_freq(words: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]:

  • Define a function called count_freq.

  • Input: list of strings.

  • Output: dictionary from string to integer.

Python
    counts = {}

  • Create an empty dictionary.

  • This will store word frequencies.

Python
    for word in words:

  • Visit each word one by one.

Python
        if word not in counts:

  • Check whether this word has been seen before.

Python
            counts[word] = 0

  • If the word is new, initialize its count to zero.

Python
        counts[word] += 1

  • Increase the count of the current word by one.

Python
    return counts

  • Return the completed frequency dictionary.

Mental model

At every moment:

  • counts[w] = how many times word w has appeared so far

Dry run

For:

Python
["cat", "dog", "cat"]

  • start: {}

  • see "cat" → not present → set 0, then add 1 → {"cat": 1}

  • see "dog" → not present → set 0, then add 1 → {"cat": 1, "dog": 1}

  • see "cat" → already present → add 1 → {"cat": 2, "dog": 1}

Complexity

  • Time: O(n)

  • Space: O(k) where k is number of distinct words

Better Python version

Python
def count_freq(words: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]:
    counts = {}

    for word in words:
        counts[word] = counts.get(word, 0) + 1

    return counts

Important line

Python
counts[word] = counts.get(word, 0) + 1

  • counts.get(word, 0) means:

    • return counts[word] if it exists

    • otherwise return 0

  • then add 1

This is the compact Pythonic version.

Problem 3: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

This is one of the first real “pattern” problems.

Problem

Given a list where prices[i] is the stock price on day i, choose one day to buy and a later day to sell to maximize profit.

Return the maximum profit.

Example:

Python
prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]

Answer:

Python
5

Because:

  • buy at 1

  • sell at 6

Brute force idea

Try every buy day with every later sell day.

That works, but is O(n^2).

We want better.

Key insight

As you scan left to right:

  • keep track of the lowest price seen so far

  • for each current price, compute the profit if you sell today

  • keep the best profit

That is invariant tracking.

Code

Python
def max_profit(prices: list[int]) -> int:
    min_price = float("inf")
    best_profit = 0

    for price in prices:
        if price < min_price:
            min_price = price

        profit = price - min_price

        if profit > best_profit:
            best_profit = profit

    return best_profit

Line-by-line explanation

Python
def max_profit(prices: list[int]) -> int:

  • Define a function named max_profit.

  • Input: list of integer prices.

  • Output: integer maximum profit.

Python
    min_price = float("inf")

  • float("inf") means positive infinity.

  • We start min_price with a huge value so that the first real price will be smaller.

  • This variable stores the smallest price seen so far.

Python
    best_profit = 0

  • This stores the best profit found so far.

  • Start at 0 because if prices only go down, best possible answer is 0.

Python
    for price in prices:

  • Scan through prices from left to right.

Python
        if price < min_price:
            min_price = price

  • If current price is the smallest seen so far, update min_price.

  • This means: “this is the best day to buy so far.”

Python
        profit = price - min_price

  • Compute profit if:

    • we bought at the cheapest day seen so far

    • and sold today

Python
        if profit > best_profit:
            best_profit = profit

  • If selling today gives a better profit than any previous answer, update best_profit.

Python
    return best_profit

  • Return the best answer after scanning everything.

Important invariant

At the start of each iteration:

  • min_price = smallest price seen up to this point

  • best_profit = best profit found so far

That invariant is the whole solution.

Dry run

For:

Python
[7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]

Start:

  • min_price = inf

  • best_profit = 0

See 7

  • 7 < inf → min_price = 7

  • profit = 7 - 7 = 0

  • best_profit = 0

See 1

  • 1 < 7 → min_price = 1

  • profit = 1 - 1 = 0

  • best_profit = 0

See 5

  • 5 < 1? no

  • profit = 5 - 1 = 4

  • best_profit = 4

See 3

  • 3 < 1? no

  • profit = 3 - 1 = 2

  • best_profit = 4

See 6

  • 6 < 1? no

  • profit = 6 - 1 = 5

  • best_profit = 5

See 4

  • 4 < 1? no

  • profit = 4 - 1 = 3

  • best_profit = 5

Return 5.

Complexity

  • Time: O(n)

  • Space: O(1)

Why this problem matters

This teaches:

  • one-pass optimization

  • keeping summary variables

  • invariants

  • refactoring brute force into linear time

That is real engineer thinking.

6. The reusable pattern template

For many one-pass problems, this template works:

Python
def solve(data):
    state = ...
    answer = ...

    for item in data:
        # maybe update state
        # compute something from current item + state
        # update answer

    return answer

Examples:

  • state = smallest so far

  • state = frequency map

  • state = running sum

  • state = current window

  • state = stack

When you get good, you stop memorizing solutions and start recognizing which state is needed.

7. Pen-and-paper drills for today

Do these without looking.

Drill 1

Write from memory:

Python
def total_sum(nums: list[int]) -> int:
    ...

Drill 2

Write from memory:

Python
def count_freq(words: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]:
    ...

Drill 3

Write from memory:

Python
def max_profit(prices: list[int]) -> int:
    ...

Drill 4

For each function, say out loud:

  • what each variable means

  • what the loop invariant is

  • the time complexity

  • the space complexity

That habit is what separates weak coders from strong ones.

8. Your first homework problem set

Solve these in this order:

  1. Find the sum of even numbers in a list

  2. Count how many times each character appears in a string

  3. Find the largest number in a list

  4. Find the second largest number in a list

  5. Contains Duplicate

  6. Majority Element

  7. Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

  8. Maximum Subarray

For the first 4, do them from scratch before touching LeetCode.

9. What I will teach you next in this curriculum

The next pack should be:

Hash maps and sets

  • why they work

  • dict vs set

  • lookup pattern

  • Two Sum

  • Contains Duplicate

  • Valid Anagram

  • line-by-line explanations again

That is the correct next step because hash-based thinking is one of the biggest jumps in DSA skill.

When you're ready, ask for Pattern Pack 2: Hash maps and sets.


You said:

what would one require to build our own agent harness

ChatGPT said:

To build your own agent harness, you need the layer that sits around the model and makes it reliable.

An agent harness is not the model itself. It is the runtime that handles:

  • prompt assembly

  • tool calling

  • state management

  • execution loop

  • memory/context injection

  • retries and error handling

  • logging, tracing, evaluation, and guardrails

Think of it as the orchestrator + runtime + safety rails for agents.

Core pieces you need

1. Model interface

A clean wrapper over the LLM.

It should hide provider-specific details and expose one consistent API like:

Python
class LLMClient:
    def complete(self, messages, tools=None, temperature=0.2):
        ...

This layer should handle:

  • model name

  • retries

  • timeouts

  • token counting

  • streaming or non-streaming

  • provider switching

Without this, everything gets tightly coupled to one API.

2. Message/state schema

You need a standard internal representation for conversations and steps.

Example:

Python
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class Message:
    role: str
    content: str

@dataclass
class ToolCall:
    name: str
    arguments: dict[str, Any]

@dataclass
class StepResult:
    thought: str | None = None
    tool_calls: list[ToolCall] = field(default_factory=list)
    final_answer: str | None = None

This matters because the harness needs to know:

  • what the agent has seen

  • what it has done

  • what tool outputs came back

  • whether it should continue or stop

3. Tool registry

Agents become useful when they can call tools.

You need:

  • a way to register tools

  • JSON-schema-like argument definitions

  • execution wrapper

  • validation

  • failure handling

Minimal example:

Python
class Tool:
    def __init__(self, name, description, schema, fn):
        self.name = name
        self.description = description
        self.schema = schema
        self.fn = fn

    def run(self, **kwargs):
        return self.fn(**kwargs)

Then a registry:

Python
class ToolRegistry:
    def __init__(self):
        self._tools = {}

    def register(self, tool: Tool):
        self._tools[tool.name] = tool

    def get(self, name: str) -> Tool:
        return self._tools[name]

You also need rules for:

  • tool timeout

  • malformed args

  • unknown tool names

  • repeated useless tool loops

4. Agent loop

This is the heart of the harness.

Basic loop:

  1. build prompt from system + user + state

  2. call model

  3. inspect output

  4. if tool requested, run tool

  5. append tool result to state

  6. call model again

  7. stop on final answer or max steps

Pseudo-code:

Python
def run_agent(user_input: str, state, llm, tools, max_steps=10):
    state.add_user_message(user_input)

    for _ in range(max_steps):
        response = llm.complete(
            messages=state.to_messages(),
            tools=tools.describe()
        )

        parsed = parse_model_output(response)

        if parsed.final_answer:
            state.add_assistant_message(parsed.final_answer)
            return parsed.final_answer

        for call in parsed.tool_calls:
            tool = tools.get(call.name)
            result = tool.run(**call.arguments)
            state.add_tool_result(call.name, result)

    raise RuntimeError("Agent exceeded max steps")

This is the minimum viable harness.

5. Prompt construction layer

You should not hardcode giant prompt strings everywhere.

You want a prompt builder that combines:

  • system instructions

  • tool descriptions

  • memory/context

  • user request

  • scratch state / prior steps

  • output format instructions

You need this layer because agent quality often depends more on prompt composition than raw model quality.

6. Output parser

The model output must be turned into something executable.

You need to parse:

  • final answer

  • tool name

  • tool arguments

  • control decisions like continue/stop

This parser must be defensive because models can produce:

  • invalid JSON

  • partially correct structures

  • hallucinated tool names

  • missing required args

So your harness needs either:

  • strict structured outputs, or

  • robust repair/parsing logic

7. Memory/context layer

You need rules for what context goes into each call.

Usually this includes:

  • current conversation

  • retrieved documents

  • prior tool results

  • task plan

  • user profile/preferences

  • summarized long history

Without memory discipline, the context window gets polluted fast.

You usually want:

  • working memory: current task state

  • episodic memory: past interactions or summaries

  • retrieval memory: docs/search results

  • tool memory: previous tool outputs

8. Stop conditions

A harness needs to know when to stop.

Typical stop conditions:

  • model produced final answer

  • max steps reached

  • repeated identical tool calls

  • no progress detected

  • tool failure threshold exceeded

This is one of the biggest differences between a demo agent and a usable one.

9. Observability

You need to see what the agent is doing.

At minimum, log:

  • prompt sent

  • model response

  • tool calls

  • tool outputs

  • latency

  • token usage

  • errors

  • final result

For serious use, also track:

  • per-step traces

  • cost by request

  • loop depth

  • failure reasons

  • hallucinated tool calls

  • retry counts

If you cannot inspect behavior, you cannot improve the harness.

10. Evaluation harness

This is what most people skip.

You need a way to test:

  • does the agent solve tasks correctly?

  • does it choose the right tools?

  • does it loop?

  • does it fail safely?

  • does it degrade under noisy input?

Useful eval categories:

  • single-tool tasks

  • multi-step tasks

  • malformed tool input

  • ambiguous user queries

  • tool outage simulation

  • adversarial prompt injection

  • long-context tasks

Without evals, every change becomes guesswork.

What a production-grade harness additionally needs

A serious version also needs:

Guardrails

  • input validation

  • output validation

  • prompt injection defenses

  • tool permission boundaries

  • unsafe action confirmation

  • sandboxing for code/file tools

Reliability

  • retries with backoff

  • circuit breakers

  • fallback models

  • idempotent tool execution

  • caching

  • resumable runs

Performance

  • parallel tool execution where safe

  • batching

  • retrieval caching

  • prompt compression

  • context summarization

Multi-agent support

If you want more than one agent, you need:

  • role definitions

  • handoff rules

  • shared state

  • supervisor logic

  • conflict resolution

But do not start here. Start with one good agent.

Minimal tech choices for a Python v0

If you want to build it yourself in Python, a clean v0 is:

  • FastAPI for API layer

  • Pydantic for schemas

  • httpx for provider/tool calls

  • SQLite/Postgres for run storage

  • Redis optional for queues/cache

  • plain Python classes for orchestration

  • structured logging

  • pytest for evals/tests

You do not need a heavy framework first.

Suggested folder structure

agent_harness/
├── app/
│   ├── api/
│   │   └── routes.py
│   ├── agents/
│   │   ├── runner.py
│   │   ├── prompts.py
│   │   ├── parser.py
│   │   └── state.py
│   ├── llm/
│   │   └── client.py
│   ├── tools/
│   │   ├── registry.py
│   │   ├── base.py
│   │   └── implementations/
│   ├── memory/
│   │   └── store.py
│   ├── evals/
│   │   └── cases.py
│   ├── observability/
│   │   └── tracing.py
│   └── main.py
├── tests/
└── pyproject.toml

What you need intellectually

Beyond code, you need clarity on these design questions:

What kind of agent is this?

  • chatbot with tools?

  • researcher?

  • coding agent?

  • workflow executor?

  • customer support agent?

What actions can it take?

Every allowed action creates risk and complexity.

What is the unit of state?

  • per turn?

  • per conversation?

  • per task?

  • per workflow run?

What does success mean?

  • correct answer?

  • correct tool selection?

  • task completion?

  • low latency?

  • low cost?

What are hard constraints?

  • must not email without approval?

  • must not write files outside sandbox?

  • must cite sources?

  • must ask before external side effects?

Those decisions shape the harness more than code does.

Practical maturity ladder

Level 1: toy harness

  • one model

  • one or two tools

  • one execution loop

  • max step count

  • simple logging

Level 2: usable harness

  • schema-validated tools

  • retries

  • persistent state

  • traces

  • eval suite

  • better prompt building

Level 3: serious harness

  • memory layers

  • guardrails

  • task recovery

  • model routing

  • async jobs

  • observability dashboard

  • offline evaluation pipeline

Biggest mistakes people make

  1. Building multi-agent first
    One agent with good tooling beats five vague agents.

  2. No state model
    Then debugging becomes impossible.

  3. No tool validation
    The model emits junk and your runtime breaks.

  4. No stop conditions
    The agent loops forever or burns tokens.

  5. No evaluation set
    You cannot tell whether improvements are real.

  6. Overusing frameworks too early
    You learn less and lose control of the core loop.

If I were building it from scratch, I would start with this exact sequence

  1. define message and tool schemas

  2. build one LLM client wrapper

  3. build one tool registry

  4. build one synchronous agent loop

  5. add parser + final answer/tool call branching

  6. add max-steps and failure handling

  7. add structured logs and traces

  8. add persistence for runs

  9. write 20 eval tasks

  10. only then add retrieval, memory, and multi-agent features

That is the correct order.

The shortest answer

To build your own agent harness, you need:

  • an LLM wrapper

  • a message/state model

  • a tool registry

  • an execution loop

  • prompt construction

  • output parsing

  • memory/context handling

  • stop conditions

  • logging/tracing

  • evaluation + guardrails

That is the real minimum.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete Python project scaffold with the exact files and starter code for a minimal but clean agent harness.


ChatGPT can make mistakes. Check important info. See Cookie Preferences.
